View Media Details     P Waves alternately compress and stretch the crustal material parallel to the direction they are propagating. S Waves cause the crustal material\u00a0to move back and forth perpendicular to the direction they are travelling. (Public domain.)     time between the two.     By looking at the amount of time between the P and S wave on a seismogram recorded on a seismograph, scientists can tell how far away the earthquake was from that location. However, they can\u2019t tell in what direction from the seismograph the earthquake was, only how far away it was. If they draw a circle on a map around the station where the\u00a0radius\u00a0of the circle is the determined distance to the earthquake, they know the earthquake lies somewhere on the circle. But where?     Scientists then use a method called\u00a0triangulation\u00a0to determine exactly where the earthquake was (see image below). It is called triangulation because a triangle has three sides, and it takes three seismographs to locate an earthquake. If you draw a circle on a map around three di\"erent seismographs where the\u00a0radius\u00a0of each is the distance from that station to the earthquake, the intersection of those three circles is the\u00a0epicenter!     Can scientists predict earthquakes?", "relevance_score": 0.6207, "source": {"document": "test.pdf", "s3_uri": "s3://groundsense-dev-documents/2026/test.pdf"}}, {"rank": 2, "content": "19/03/26, 17:51The Science of Earthquakes | U.S. Geological Survey     Page 1 of 8https://www.usgs.gov/programs/earthquake-hazards/science-earthquakes     The Science of Earthquakes By Earthquake Hazards Program     HOME     EARTHQUA KES     HAZARDS     SCIENCE     MONITORIN G     EDUCATION     DATA     MAPS     MULTIMEDI A     PUBLICATIO NS     SOFTWARE     NEWS     Originally written by Lisa Wald (U.S. Geological Survey)\u00a0for \u201cThe Green Frog News\u201d     Sources/Usage: Public Domain. View Media Details     A normal (dip-slip) fault is an inclined fracture\u00a0where the rock\u00a0mass\u00a0above an inclined fault moves down (Public domain.)     What is an earthquake?\u00a0 An\u00a0earthquake\u00a0is what happens when two blocks of the earth suddenly slip past one another. The surface where they slip is called the\u00a0fault\u00a0or\u00a0fault plane. The location below the earth\u2019s surface where the earthquake starts is called the\u00a0hypocenter, and the location directly above it on the surface of the earth is called the\u00a0epicenter.     Sometimes an earthquake has\u00a0foreshocks. The recording they make is called a\u00a0seismogram.\u00a0The seismograph has a base that sets firmly in the ground, and           https://www.usgs.gov/media/images/tectonic-plates-earth         https://www.usgs.gov/media/images/tectonic-plates-earth19/03/26, 17:51The Science of Earthquakes | U.S. Geological Survey     Page 4 of 8https://www.usgs.gov/programs/earthquake-hazards/science-earthquakes     Sources/Usage: Public Domain. View Media Details     The cartoon sketch of the seismograph shows how the insrument shakes with the earth below it, but the recording device remains stationary (instead of the other way around). (Public domain.)     Sources/Usage: Public Domain. View Media Details     An example of a seismic wave with the P wave and S wave labeled. (Public domain.)     a heavy weight that hangs free. When an earthquake causes the ground to shake, the base of the seismograph shakes too, but the hanging weight does not. Instead the spring or string that it is hanging from absorbs all the movement. The di\"erence in position between the shaking part of the seismograph and the motionless part is what is recorded.     How do scientists measure the size of earthquakes?", "relevance_score": 0.6063, "source": {"document": "test.pdf", "s3_uri": "s3://groundsense-dev-documents/2026/test.pdf"}}]}
